title: Your First Inquiry: Comparing Creation Stories Across Traditions
description: Explore how different religious traditions answer the same fundamental question -- How did the world begin and what is the human place in it? -- through comparative reading of creation narratives.

steps.instruction: Read the concept on sacred stories to understand the difference between myth as sacred narrative (meaning-making) versus myth as false belief. Then read the Genesis creation account (Genesis 1:1-2:3) with this lens: what does it say about the nature of the world and the role of humans in it?
steps.expectedOutcome: You can identify the key theological claims in Genesis beyond the literal cosmological description: ordered goodness, human stewardship, Sabbath rest
steps.hint: Read for the story's answers to: What kind of world is this? What are humans for? What is the relationship between creator and creation?

steps.instruction: Read a summary of the Hindu Rigveda creation hymn (Nasadiya Sukta / Hymn of Creation). This text is remarkable for its philosophical uncertainty about origins. Compare it to Genesis: how does the Hindu approach to the question differ from the Abrahamic approach?
steps.expectedOutcome: You notice that the Rigveda ends with 'perhaps even the gods don't know' -- a radically different epistemological stance than Genesis's confident account
steps.hint: The Nasadiya Sukta is one of the oldest texts in any language to seriously question whether anyone can know the ultimate origin of things

steps.instruction: Read about world religions -- specifically the Buddhist teaching on dependent origination (pratityasamutpada). Buddhism does not teach a creation story in the usual sense. Why not? What does Buddhism say instead about the origin of suffering and the nature of existence?
steps.expectedOutcome: You understand that Buddhism sidesteps cosmological questions as 'unanswered questions' (avyakata) -- not because they don't matter but because answering them doesn't reduce suffering

steps.instruction: Read about religious ethics and ritual practice. Then consider: what does each tradition's creation story imply about human responsibility to care for the environment? Compare the Abrahamic 'stewardship,' the Hindu 'dharmic responsibility,' and the Indigenous 'reciprocal relationship with the land.'
steps.expectedOutcome: You can trace how cosmological claims generate ethical implications -- a creation story is also a statement about how to live

steps.instruction: Write a brief reflection: What surprised you in the comparison? Which approach resonates with you personally, and why? What questions remain? Read about theodicy -- does any creation story you explored adequately address the problem of why suffering exists?
steps.expectedOutcome: You have engaged comparatively with at least three traditions' answers to foundational questions about existence, and identified genuine philosophical questions the narratives raise
